metadata.title: Build any shape out of pure waves
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=individual mode / eigenstate; CRIMSON=partial sum before convergence; GOLD=target shape fill; SLATE=structure/axes
metadata.note: Tie each Fourier mode to a QM eigenstate and each coefficient to a Born-rule projection amplitude. 10-beat slate cut, no STILL·ai beats.

beats.narration_text: Give me enough sine waves and I can draw you anything — even a square corner.
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow QUANTUM MECHANICS, headline 'Give me enough sine
waves and I'll draw
you anything'

beats.narration_text: A wavefunction in a box is just a list of amplitudes — one for each standing wave. Why do those amplitudes look exactly like the Born rule probability weights? What does Fourier series have to do with quantum measurement?
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow THE QUESTION, headline 'Why do Fourier coefficients
look like Born-rule
probability amplitudes?'

beats.narration_text: Start with the simplest case: a particle in a box. The allowed energy states are sine waves. The first mode is a single arch. The second fits two arches into the same box. The third fits three. Each mode is a standing wave with a definite energy.
beats.visual: Axes on left. Plot mode n=1 (TEAL), then n=2 (TEAL), then n=3 (TEAL), stacked vertically on the right side. Label each with its energy E_n = n^2 * E_1.

beats.narration_text: Now suppose the particle starts in some arbitrary shape — say, a triangular spike near the center of the box. That shape is not one of the energy modes. But it can be built from a sum of modes, each with a coefficient c sub n. The coefficient measures how much of mode n lives in the initial shape.
beats.visual: Left panel: target triangular spike (GOLD filled). Right panel: three TEAL sine modes n=1,2,3 with coefficient bars c_1 > c_2 > c_3 beneath each. Arrow from target to mode set.

beats.narration_text: Here is where Fourier and quantum mechanics click together. To find c sub n, you project the target shape onto mode n — you multiply them together and integrate. That is identically the Born rule inner product: c sub n equals the integral of phi sub n star times psi. If you measure energy, the probability of getting E sub n is just the modulus squared of c sub n.
beats.visual: Equation display: c_n = <phi_n | psi> = integral phi_n*(x) psi(x) dx. Below: P(E_n) = |c_n|^2. TEAL for c_n equation, CRIMSON for P(E_n) = |c_n|^2.

beats.narration_text: Watch the partial sum accumulate. First mode: a broad arch that roughly follows the spike. Add the third mode and the sum sharpens. The fifth mode sharpens it further. By five modes the partial sum is already recognizably the triangular spike.
beats.visual: Running sum plot: show target (GOLD faint), partial sum after n=1 (CRIMSON), after n=3, n=5 (transitioning CRIMSON to TEAL as it converges). All on same axes.

beats.narration_text: The same logic applies to the square wave — the iconic Fourier example. The first five modes produce the staircase approximation with the Gibbs ringing at the corners. The modes never perfectly reproduce the discontinuous jump, but the sum converges in every other sense.
beats.visual: New axes: target square wave (GOLD faint), partial sum at n=1,3,5 (odd modes only, CRIMSON to TEAL). Label the Gibbs overshoot with a SLATE bracket.

beats.narration_text: In quantum mechanics this is the completeness statement: any normalizable wavefunction can be written as a sum over energy eigenstates. The eigenstates form a basis, and the coefficients are the Born-rule amplitudes. Fourier series is not an analogy for quantum mechanics — it is quantum mechanics, restricted to a box.
beats.visual: Completeness equation: psi = sum_n c_n phi_n. Below: normalization sum_n |c_n|^2 = 1. TEAL for completeness, CRIMSON for normalization constraint. Box outline (SLATE) around both.

beats.narration_text: Concrete numbers. Start with the triangular spike: psi of x equals x on the left half, and L minus x on the right half. The coefficients work out to proportional to 8 over n squared pi squared times the sine of n pi over 2. That is zero for every even n. Only odd modes contribute. The probability of measuring energy E sub 1 is about 99 percent — nearly the whole spike is the ground state.
beats.visual: Table: n=1, c_1^2 approx 0.99; n=3, c_3^2 approx 0.01; n=5, c_5^2 approx 0.002. TEAL for n=1 row (dominant), CRIMSON for n=3,5 rows. Bar chart on right matching.

beats.narration_text: Fourier modes are energy eigenstates. Fourier coefficients are Born-rule amplitudes. Building a shape from sines is quantum superposition.
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow QUANTUM MECHANICS, headline 'Fourier modes = eigenstates.
Coefficients = amplitudes.
Building shapes = superposition.'

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Math for Quantum Physics series.
